retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Primary bone cancer remains uncommon, accounting for 0.2% of all malignancies and 5% of childhood malignancies. In the United States, an estimated 3600 new cases of primary bone cancer were diagnosed in 2020, with 1720 deaths, making up 0.3% of all cancer deaths. The National Cancer Institute data shows that chondrosarcoma (40%) is most prevalent in adults, followed by osteosarcoma (28%). In children and adolescents, osteosarcoma (56%) is the most common, with Ewing sarcoma (34%) second. Chordoma, undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma, adamantinoma, fibrosarcoma, and giant cell tumor of the bone are also types of primary bone cancer; however, they are fewer in number. Primary bone cancer has a male predominance, with a worldwide osteosarcoma male-to-female ratio of 1.43 to 1. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Managing primary bone cancer requires a multidisciplinary approach by a specialist bone cancer center, including staff trained in providing age-appropriate care to children or adolescents. Management depends on several factors, including tumor type, stage and grade, and patient preference. Surgical excision remains the cornerstone of primary bone cancer treatment. Neoadjuvant and adjuvant chemotherapy are also commonplace in the management, with radiotherapy used in specific cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bones -- Clinical Significance -- Osteosarcoma
retrieved_docs.content: Osteosarcoma is a highly aggressive bone malignancy, the 3rd most common adolescent cancer after leukemia and lymphoma. [20] This condition has a bimodal age distribution globally, frequently affecting patients in the 2nd to 3rd and 7th to 8th decades of life. [21] Osteosarcoma involves malignant bone-forming primitive mesenchymal cells. Primary osteosarcoma most commonly affects the long bones, particularly the proximal humerus and tibia and distal femur. Symptoms include nighttime pain, reduced range of motion, and swelling of the nearby joints. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Osteosarcoma accounts for approximately 2.4% of pediatric cancers, making it the eighth most common childhood malignancy. Leukemia is the most common (30%), followed by malignancies of the central nervous system (22.3%), neuroblastoma (7.3%), Wilms tumor (5.6%), non-Hodgkin lymphoma (4.5%), rhabdomyosarcoma (3.1%), and retinoblastoma (2.8%). [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Though primary bone cancer is most often idiopathic, risk factors also play a role in developing this cancer. Genetic factors are linked. Germline abnormalities in hereditary cancer predisposition syndromes have an increased risk of later developing bone cancer through the downregulation of tumor suppressor genes or upregulation of oncogenes. The TP53 tumor suppressor gene is often altered in Li-Fraumeni syndrome, putting patients at an increased risk of developing osteosarcoma. Similarly, a mutation in the Rb1 gene leading to hereditary retinoblastoma is linked to osteosarcoma. Werner and Rothmund-Thomson syndromes are also linked to an increased risk of developing osteosarcoma. Previous treatment for cancer with radiotherapy is linked to an increased risk of developing primary bone cancer in later life, particularly when exposed to ionizing radiation in childhood.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary pediatric bone malignancy, derived from primitive bone-forming (osteoid producing) mesenchymal cells. It occurs in primary (no underlying bone pathology) and secondary forms (underlying pathology which has undergone malignant degeneration/conversion), accounting for approximately 20% of all primary bone tumors. Osteosarcoma is highly heterogeneous in its manifestation, which permits division into several subtypes according to the degree of differentiation, location within the bone, and histological variation. These subtypes vary in imaging appearance, demographics, and biological behavior. With the ceaseless work of numerous medical, surgical, and scientific professionals, treatment options and survivability have vastly improved in recent years. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Unicameral Bone Cyst -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Unicameral bone cysts are most commonly found in adolescents from birth to 20 years of age. They have a predilection for males (3:1, male:female). Unicameral bone cysts make up 3% of the primary bone lesions. The actual incidence is not known as many of these are never discovered [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Cyst -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: ABC is rarer, at 1.4 per 10000 population annually, and accounts for 9.1% of all bone tumors. [5] There is a female preponderance, and 80% of these lesions occur in the second decade. Most occur in patients younger than 20 years. It is rare after 30 years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Primary bone cancer is a rare malignant tumor of the bone originating from primitive mesenchymal cells. This condition accounts for around 0.2% of all malignancies worldwide and is idiopathic in most cases. Multiple subtypes are prevalent, with osteosarcoma, chondrosarcoma, and Ewing sarcoma being the most common. Each varies in demographics, imaging appearance, and biological behavior. They are frequently aggressive and require early diagnosis, utilizing imaging and tissue biopsy. Surgical excision remains the mainstay of curative treatment, with chemotherapy and radiotherapy used in conjunction. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ewing sarcoma is an aggressive tumor of childhood and adolescence, most commonly occurring in the bone but also seen in soft tissues. The peak incidence is at 15 years, and the men-to-women ratio is 5:1. The most frequent sites involved are long bones in the lower limb, pelvic bones, and the axial skeleton (ribs and vertebral column). Ewing sarcoma develops characteristically at the diaphysis, in contrast to the pattern seen in osteosarcoma. Ewing sarcoma is genetically well described, with characteristic chromosomal translocations identified. The translocation leads to fusing an FET protein to an ETS transcription factor, most commonly FLI1 (>85% of cases). The result is the formation of fusion proteins that deregulate downstream genes, altering cell behavior. [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Osteosarcoma frequently occurs near the metaphysis of the long bones of the appendicular skeleton. Rapid bone growth predisposes to the development of osteosarcoma; occurrence is most common during the pubertal growth spurt and near the growing physis. [15] High-grade intramedullary osteosarcoma comprises approximately 80% of all osteosarcomas; 42% occur in the femur, 19% in the tibia, and 10% in the humerus. Between 75% and 90% of these long-bone tumors occur near the distal femoral, proximal tibial, or proximal humeral physis. Other commonly encountered anatomical sites for osteosarcoma development include the skull, jaw, and pelvis, primarily the ilium. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of osteosarcoma is about 3.4 cases per million people per year. [14] Osteosarcomas constitute <1% of all newly diagnosed malignancies in adults and nearly 4% of all newly diagnosed malignancies in children. [4] Excluding hematological malignancies, osteosarcoma is the most commonly diagnosed malignancy in adolescents, with an incidence of 4.4 per million per year. [2] Osteosarcoma does have a slightly increased incidence in males versus females. [7] Approximately 20% of osteosarcoma are metastatic at presentation. Between 60% and 70% of metastatic disease is to the lungs; another 20% to 30% of metastatic lesions are skip or distant bony metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Primary tumors usually occur in the metaphysis of long bones and have a marked predilection for the knee, with nearly 60% occurring at this location. Children and adolescents account for the vast majority of those affected by this condition. [5] Secondary tumors have a much wider distribution, reflecting the varied nature of their underlying predisposing condition. They almost always occur in the adult population. Notably, they feature a higher incidence in flat bones, the pelvis in particular (a common location of Paget disease). [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Enchondroma -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: These lesions may disrupt normal bone development in children.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although the quality of life of patients affected by osteosarcoma has significantly improved over the last few decades, its etiology remains obscure. Studies aiming to determine the causes of osteosarcoma have classically focused on multiple factors, including genetics, epidemiology, and the environment. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Primary osteosarcoma is primarily a lesion of childhood and adolescence with a marked propensity for the knee. [1] Secondary osteosarcoma reflects the varied nature of the predisposing condition with a broader age distribution, primarily within adulthood. Secondary osteosarcoma is much more likely to occur in flat bones, including the pelvis; this is likely due to the causative effect of Paget's disease of bone, which shares a similar anatomical preference. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1898
retrieved_docs.content: The CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE MUSCULOSKELETAL SYSTEM AND SOFT TISSUES encompasses specific subcategories for metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head and for metastatic malignant neoplasm to the supraclavicular region. By defining these child categories, the classification aids clinicians in recognizing patterns of metastasis from musculoskeletal and soft‑tissue tumors to distinct anatomical sites, which is essential for accurate staging, targeted diagnostic work‑up, and formulation of appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1192
retrieved_docs.content: The CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE MUSCULOSKELETAL SYSTEM AND SOFT TISSUES encompasses specific subcategories for metastatic malignant neoplasms, including Metastatic malignant neoplasm to head, Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular region, and the Arabic‑language category نقائل إلى الرقبة (metastatic neoplasm to the neck). These child categories delineate common distant sites to which musculoskeletal and soft‑tissue malignancies may spread, aiding clinicians in organizing disease staging, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and planning targeted therapeutic strategies for patients with metastatic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 179
retrieved_docs.content: the primary lesion’s nature is unclear. Similarly, “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to thymus” carries the same “النقيلة” relationship, reinforcing the link between thymic metastasis and the general concept of metastatic disease. Clinical associations are highlighted by the relationship between “kanker; metastase” and “سرطان الغدة الدرقية” (thyroid cancer), indicating that metastatic cancer is clinically associated with thyroid malignancy. Moreover, “سرطان الغدة الدرقية” is identified as a child of “ورم صماوي خبيث” (malignant tumor), situating thyroid cancer within the broader class of malignant neoplasms. Collectively, these interconnections illustrate how metastatic cancer entities serve as parent concepts for organ‑specific metastatic sites (skin, bone, respiratory/intrathoracic organs, thymus, endocrine glands) and how primary malignant neoplasms—whether well‑defined or of uncertain benign‑malignant status—relate to metastatic potential. Understanding this hierarchical and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is a malignant condition that involves the skeletal system and its supporting connective tissues. It has a broader relationship with ورم العظام, indicating that bone tumors (ورم العظام) are encompassed within this wider category of cancer. Recognizing that ورم العظام represents a specific manifestation of cancer of bone and connective tissue aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, appropriate classification, and targeted therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are each examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which constitute pathological processes. These entities represent malignant tumor growth arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head, the nasal bone, and the thymus—characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of cancerous cells. As pathological processes, they require oncologic assessment, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies to manage the aggressive nature of the malignancy in each location.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2192
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face is a specific type of neoplasm of bone of the face, representing a malignant tumor that originates in the facial bones. This relationship indicates that the broader category “neoplasm of bone of the face” encompasses the more specific entity “primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face,” clarifying the hierarchical classification of facial bone tumors for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone, metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone (diagnosis) are each classified as malignant neoplasms of the ethmoid bone, frontal bone, and temporal bone respectively. These site‑specific malignant bone tumors are encompassed within the broader diagnostic category “cancer of bone and connective tissue,” which is mapped to each of the three bone‑specific malignant neoplasms. Consequently, the three metastatic bone cancers are recognized as particular manifestations of bone and connective tissue cancer, highlighting their shared pathology while preserving their distinct anatomical locations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2655
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of bone cancer. The identification of a malignant cellular infiltrate reflects the invasion of cancerous cells into bone tissue, while a secondary lesion denotes additional disease sites that arise as the primary bone tumor progresses. Recognizing these findings together aids clinicians in confirming the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم, assessing disease extent, and guiding appropriate therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cells that define the disease known as سرطانة العظم (bone cancer).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Classification of Neoplasms of the Musculoskeletal System and Soft Tissues is organized such that it includes as a child the entity “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular region” and is itself positioned under the broader entity “malignant neoplasm of vomer”. Within this framework, each metastatic malignant neoplasm to a specific cranial bone—“Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone”, “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone”, “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to occipital bone”, “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to parietal bone”, “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone”, and “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to vomer”—is defined as an instance (“isa”) of the corresponding “malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone”, “malignant neoplasm of frontal bone”, “malignant neoplasm of occipital bone”, “malignant neoplasm of parietal bone”, “malignant neoplasm of temporal bone (diagnosis)”, and “malignant neoplasm of vomer”, respectively. The overarching category

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1119
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of frontal bone, and Metastatic malignant neoplasm to parietal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of parietal bone. Both of these site‑specific malignant neoplasms are encompassed by the broader diagnostic category cancer of bone and connective tissue, which is also referred to in Arabic as ورم العظام. By mapping the malignant neoplasms of the frontal and parietal bones to cancer of bone and connective tissue, clinicians can recognize these metastatic lesions as part of the overall spectrum of bone‑related cancers, facilitating accurate classification, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

The hierarchy further connects organ‑specific primary tumors to their metastatic potential. “Maligne neoplasma van bot en gewrichtskraakbeen, niet gespecificeerd” (unspecified malignant neoplasm of bone and joint cartilage) is linked to the broader category “Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant,” indicating uncertainty about its benign‑malignant status, and it also has “انتشار إلى العظم” as a child, emphasizing bone as a common site of spread for this tumor type. The ambiguous neoplasm category (“Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant”) is associated with the Arabic term “النقيلة,” which denotes metastasis, underscoring the clinical relevance of metastatic behavior even when the primary lesion’s nature is unclear. Similarly, “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to thymus” carries the same “النقيلة” relationship, reinforcing the link between thymic metastasis and the general

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: is a common histopathologic feature across kidney and renal‑pelvis cancer, ovarian cancer, unspecified primary cancers, pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي), bone cancer and other organ‑specific tumors. The category “Malignant neoplasm of other and unspecified sites” connects to an unspecified malignant neoplasm of bone and joint cartilage, which in turn links to “Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant” (النقيلة) and its child “انتشار إلى العظم” (spread to bone), illustrating the propensity for skeletal metastasis. Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant (النقيلة) represents an indeterminate neoplastic process that is linked to a wide spectrum of metastatic disease concepts. Metastatic cancer (kanker; metastase) is classified as a secondary malignancy, child of bone metastasis (انتشار إلى العظم) and malignant neoplasm without specification of site, and is clinically associated with thyroid cancer (سرطان الغدة الدرقية) and ovarian metastases (نقائل إلى المبيض).
